title: Miniature accordion book insert
subtitle: Folding a small accordion book to tip into a journal or notebook
excerpt: A miniature accordion book folds to the size of a journal page but opens out to a continuous strip four to eight times as long. Tipped into a journal, it functions as an expandable insert for a long timeline, a map, a folded letter, or a storyboard. This tutorial covers the folding and tipping-in sequence.
sourceNotes: Keith Smith, Non-Adhesive Binding: Books Without Paste or Glue (first edition, public domain portions); the accordion structure as documented in Japanese and Western artists' book traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A single section of an accordion fold, bounded on each side by a valley or mountain fold. Each panel is one page of the expanded book.
The alternating fold directions in an accordion. A valley fold opens toward the viewer; a mountain fold opens away. The two alternate throughout the strip to create the zig-zag structure.

Attaching a folded insert to a journal page by gluing a narrow strip (the tip) along one folded edge, then pressing it flat against the page. The insert remains attached at one edge and opens freely from the other.

content.text: A miniature accordion begins as a long strip of paper scored and folded into alternating
the first panel, the accordion sits flat when closed and extends out fully when opened.

items.name: Paper strip
items.notes: For an A5 journal, cut a strip 148 mm wide (the height of the journal page) and as long as the number of panels times the panel width. For 6 panels of 90 mm each, the strip is 148 x 540 mm. Cartridge paper or light card (120 to 160 gsm) folds cleanly without cracking.

items.name: Bone folder
items.notes: For scoring and sharpening every fold.

items.name: Metal ruler and pencil
items.notes: For marking panel widths before scoring.

items.name: Scissors
items.notes: For trimming the strip to exact length.

content.text: Mark the panel divisions across the strip in pencil, measuring the panel width at regular intervals. Score each mark with the bone folder against the metal ruler. Score lightly on one side for valley folds, then flip the strip and score the alternate lines from the other side for mountain folds. Scoring from the outside of the intended fold prevents cracking.

Fold along each scored line, alternating direction to create the zig-zag. Sharpen each fold with the bone folder, pressing firmly along the full length of the crease. Compress the folded accordion under a weight for ten minutes to set the folds.

Apply glue stick to a 10 mm strip along the outer face of the first panel. Press the glued edge against the journal page and hold for one minute. Allow the glue to set before opening the accordion fully. The insert should open freely from the journal and lie flat when extended.
